lessonTitle: Comparing University Costs — EU, UK, US, and East Asia
concept: The same student studying the same subject can pay five times more or less depending entirely on which country they choose.

hook.fact: Same degree. Same subject. One student pays $13,000 total per year. Another pays $77,000.
hook.question: What is the only difference between them?

conceptCards.title: Card 1
conceptCards.body: The answer is destination. US private universities charge $35,000–$65,000 tuition per year for international students. German public universities charge approximately €0–€500 per semester. Same education. Radically different cost.

conceptCards.title: Card 2
conceptCards.body: The US is the most expensive destination because most top universities are privately funded — they earn their revenue almost entirely from tuition. There is no government cap on what they can charge international students.

conceptCards.title: Card 3
conceptCards.body: The UK charges international students market-rate fees (£15,000–£38,000/year) rather than the domestic cap of £9,250. The upside: most UK undergraduate degrees are three years, not four, which cuts total costs by 25%.

conceptCards.title: Card 4
conceptCards.body: EU countries vary widely. Germany, France, and Norway charge near-zero tuition even to international students — their universities are government-funded. The Netherlands and Ireland charge moderate market rates (€8,000–€20,000). Always check by country, not just "EU".

conceptCards.title: Card 5
conceptCards.body: In East Asia: Japan's national universities charge ~¥536,000/year (~$3,500) — among the cheapest quality universities globally. Singapore's NUS and NTU are internationally competitive and priced accordingly at S$17,000–S$37,000. Hong Kong sits between the two.

conceptCards.title: Card 6
conceptCards.body: Now test this: The four-year cumulative gap between a US private university (~$308,000) and a low-fee EU destination (~$52,000) is over $250,000. Two students, same subject, same ambitions — one graduates with a quarter-million dollars more in financial freedom.

questions.prompt: Why do US private universities charge so much more than EU public universities?
options: US universities are privately funded and earn revenue primarily through tuition
US degrees are internationally more respected
questions.explanation: "US private universities receive little government funding — tuition is how they fund themselves, with no government-imposed cap."

questions.statement: A UK degree typically costs more in total than a US degree because UK tuition is expensive.
questions.whyPrompt: Why is that true or false?
questions.explanation: "UK degrees are usually 3 years vs 4 years in the US. Even at higher annual fees, the shorter duration means total UK costs are often significantly lower than a US equivalent."

questions.prompt: Match each destination to its tuition model:

pairs.right: Market-rate fees for international students, 3-year degrees
High tuition, privately funded, no government cap

questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.sentence: "The cumulative four-year cost gap between a US private university and a low-fee EU destination can exceed ___."
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.prompt: Which EU country offers near-zero tuition even to non-EU international students?
questions.explanation: "German public universities charge only a nominal semester contribution (~€350), not full tuition — regardless of nationality."

questions.sentence: Complete: "NYU total cost ≈ $___. King's total cost ≈ $___. King's is approximately $___ cheaper."
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.scenario: Sofia's university comparison table shows: "EU universities are all cheap. I'll pick the one in the Netherlands since it teaches in English."
options: English-language programmes don't exist in the Netherlands
She assumed all EU universities are near-zero tuition — the Netherlands charges
Her living cost estimate is too high
questions.explanation: "Not all EU countries are the same. Germany and France charge near zero; the Netherlands charges €8,000–€20,000. Sofia's 'cheap EU' assumption could cost her €40,000+ over three years."

tasks.scenario: Kai has been accepted to four universities. Rank them by total degree cost (cheapest to most expensive):
tasks.explanation: "Germany's near-zero tuition makes it the cheapest despite reasonable living costs. Michigan's high annual fees compound dramatically over four years."

tasks.scenario: Sofia's parents will fund her degree but have told her: "We can contribute $80,000 total."
tasks.prompt: Which can she fund within her parents' contribution (plus her own savings of $20,000)?
tasks.explanation: "NUS requires $140,000 — $40,000 more than Sofia and her parents can cover. Amsterdam at ~€72,000 (~$90,000) fits within their combined $100,000. Destination is not just a preference — it is a financial constraint."

lockIn.takeaway: Where you study can matter more to your total cost than any other single decision — the gap between destinations

reviewQueueEntry.definition: Destination cost gap anchors better education money decisions.
reviewQueueEntry.quickRecallPrompt: What does Destination cost gap include beyond tuition?
